concepts.title: Equations in different forms
concepts.conceptBody:
Equation of tangent in various forms

Type 1 : Point Form  

The equation of tangent to parabola at point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is given by $\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}}=2\mathbf{a}\left( \text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

Logic : for parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$

Slope at point $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)$ is given by

$2y\left( \frac{dy}{dx} \right)=4a\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{4a}{2y}=\frac{2a}{y}$

And ${{\left. \frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \right]}_{{{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}}}}=\frac{2\text{a}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Slope of tangent at $\left( {{\text{x}}_{1}}{{\text{y}}_{1}} \right)=\frac{2\text{a}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ equation tangent becomes

$\text{y}-{{\text{y}}_{1}}=\frac{2\text{a}}{{{\text{y}}_{1}}}\left( \text{x}-{{\text{x}}_{1}} \right)$

Solving this we get the required equation.

TRICK : This form can also be obtained by replacing ${{\text{x}}^{2}}$ by $\text{x}{{\text{x}}_{1}},{{\text{y}}^{2}}$ by $\text{y}{{\text{y}}_{1}},\text{x}$ by $\frac{\text{x}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}}{2},\text{y}$

by $\frac{\text{y}+{{\text{y}}_{1}}}{2}$ and xy by $\frac{\text{x}{{\text{y}}_{1}}+{{\text{x}}_{1}}\text{y}}{2}$ in general equation of parabola.

Type 2 : Parametric Form 

The equation of tangent in parametric form given the parametric point $\left( a{{t}^{2}},2at \right)$ on

parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ is

$\text{ty}=\text{x}+\text{a}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$

just replace $\text{x}$ by $\text{a}{{\text{t}}^{2}}$ and $\text{y}$ bt $2\text{at}$ in point form to get the answer

Type 3 : Slope Form

This one we have already when we discuss the condition for tangency i.e.

$\text{y}=\text{mx}+\frac{\text{a}}{\text{m}}$

concepts.title: Point of intersection of Tangent
concepts.conceptBody:
Point of intersection of tangents

Point of intersection of tangents at the points ' ${{t}_{1}}$ ' and ' ${{t}_{2}}$ ' is $\left( a{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}},a\left( {{t}_{1}}+{{t}_{2}} \right) \right)$.

 

NOTE : 

  1. The Geometric mean of the $\text{x}$ co-ordinates of $\text{P}$ and $Q$ (i.e., $\sqrt{at_{1}^{2}\times at_{2}^{2}}=a{{t}_{1}}{{t}_{2}}$ ) is the $x$ co-ordinate of the point of intersection of tangents at $\text{P}$ and $\text{Q}$ on the parabola.
  2. The Arithmetic mean of the y-co-ordinates of $P$ and Q $\left( i.e.,\,\frac{2a{{t}_{1}}+2a{{t}_{2}}}{2}=a({{t}_{1}}+{{t}_{2}}) \right)$ is the $y$-coordinate of the point of intersection of tangents at $\text{P}$ and $\text{Q}$ on the parabola.

concepts.title: Number of tangents to a parabola
concepts.conceptBody:
Number of tangents drawn from a point to a parabola

Theorem : Two tangents can be drawn from a point to a parabola. The two tangents are

real and distinct or coincident or imaginary according as the given point

lies outside, on or inside the parabola.

Proof : Let the parabola be ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$ and $\text{P}\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$ be a point.

The equation of any tangent to the given parabola is

$\text{y}=\text{mx}+\frac{\text{a}}{\text{m}}$

If it passes through $\left( \text{h},\text{k} \right)$ then

$\begin{array}{l} \text{k} =\text{mh}+\frac{\text{a}}{\text{m}} \\ \Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{m}}^{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}-\text{mk}+\text{a} =0 \\ \end{array}$

This equation, being a quadratic in $\text{m}$, gives two values of $\text{m}$. Corresponding to

each of these values of $\text{m}$ there is a tangent to the parabola.

Thus, two tangents can be drawn from a point to a parabola.

The tangents are real and distinct, coincident or imaginary according as the roots

of quadratic in $\text{m}$ are real and distinct, equal or imaginary i.e. according as

${{\text{k}}^{2}}-4\text{ah}>\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ or }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }<0$

i.e. $\left( h,k \right)$ lies outside or on or side the parabola ${{y}^{2}}=4ax$.

concepts.title: Length of Tangent  to parabola
concepts.conceptBody:
Lengths of tangent, subtangent, normal and subnormal

Let the parabola ${{\text{y}}^{2}}=4\text{ax}$. Let tangent and normal at $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ meet the axis of parabola at $\text{T}$ and $\text{G}$ respectively and let tangent at $\text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right)$ makes angle $\theta $ with the +ve direction

of $\text{x}$-axis.

Then $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ PT $=$ Length of Tangent

PG = Length of Normal

$\text{TN}=$ Length of Subtangent

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ NG}=$ Length of Subnormal

If $\text{A}\left( 0,0 \right)$ is the vertex of the parabola

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}} \therefore & \text{PN} =\text{y} \\ {} & \text{PT}=\text{PN} \operatorname{~cosec} \theta =\text{y} \operatorname{~cosec} \theta \\ {} & \text{PG}=\text{PN} \operatorname{~cosec} \left( 90-\theta \right)=\text{y} \sec\theta \\ \text{ and } & \text{TN}=\text{PN} ~\cot\theta =\text{y} ~\cot\theta \\ {} & \text{NG}=\text{PN} ~\cot\left( 90-\theta \right)=\text{y} \tan \theta \\ \end{array}$

where $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ tan}\theta =\frac{2\text{a}}{\text{y}}=\text{m}$ [slope of tangent at $\left. \text{P}\left( \text{x},\text{y} \right) \right]$
